title: ER / EER Model
description:
**Aim:** Design an Entity-Relationship (ER) / Extended Entity-Relationship (EER) Model.

**Objective:**
- To understand entities, attributes, and relationships.
- To design ER/EER diagram for real-world problems.

**Theory:**
An Entity-Relationship (ER) Model is used for database design. 
- **Entity:** Real-world object (Student, Employee)
- **Attribute:** Property (Name, ID)
- **Relationship:** Connection between entities

**EER Model includes:**
- **Generalization:** A bottom-up method used to combine lower-level entities into a higher-level superclass. Example: Student (Roll_No, Name) and Patient (PId, Name) can be generalized into a Person (Name, Mob_No).
- **Specialization:** A top-down method where a higher-level entity is split into two or more lower-level entities. Example: Account can be specialized into Current_Acc and Savings_Acc.
- **Aggregation:** Concept where a relationship between 2 different entities is considered as a single entity. Example: WORKS_FOR relationship between EMPLOYEE and PROJECT aggregated into a single entity to relate with MACHINERY using REQUIRE relationship.

**Conclusion:** ER/EER modeling helps in structured database design.
code:
-- Conceptual Design Step
-- 1. Identify entities (Student, Course, Department)
-- 2. Define attributes (Name, ID, DateOfBirth)
-- 3. Identify relationships (EnrolledIn, BelongsTo)
-- 4. Apply EER concepts (Specialization: GraduateStudent vs UndergradStudent)
output:
Database model designed successfully. 
Entities Identified: Student, Course, Department.
Relationships: EnrolledIn (1:N), BelongsTo (M:N).
EER Specialization applied: Person -> {Student, Instructor}.
